So like, if you boiled it down into one, the thing that fascinates I think more, that I hear, fascinates more people at some point about me than anything else. So the legend is how he's able to do everything exactly his way, the autonomy story. Now there's subsections and subsections of that, but there's a legendary component to that. So, and the best thing about legend is that it sort of precedes you. So it allows you to have influence without having to work at having influence when you are known for certain things. So as an example, tell me what you know about Wyatt Earp. Who knows something about Wyatt Earp? Okay, quick draw. Do you know any more than that? But quick draw. So you don't know like how quick or quickest in the west or quickest at its time or... Well, he was a survivor so he had to be quicker than everybody else in Corral. And you said okay Corral. And what do you know about the fight at the okay Corral? He and his brothers fought the Clantons at the okay Corral and they beat them. Okay. Did he fight to the end? Like, were the last guys standing? Last man standing? Okay. What else do you know? Anything? Well, you're actually getting closer to truth, but you're still actually a ways away. Okay. All right. Anybody else? The law man that cleaned up the west. Okay. Anybody else have like anything floating around in their head about Wyatt Earp? Anybody else? Would you agree probably that if we played the game with most people we'd get pretty much the same answers? I mean, I think they're representative, don't you? Law man who cleaned up the west, fastest gun in the west. Everybody pretty much has him as a law man. Actually, he had great success as an entrepreneur for a brief period of time. So here's the wider facts. First of all, he's actually started out as a criminal. And he was arrested and jailed in 1871 as a horse thief. His friends, based on their description, as they were loyal to him because of his strong strength of character, but that attracted people from both sides of the law. So Doc Holliday, as you pointed out, of course, was a complete criminal and was intensely loyal to Earp. And politicians and lawmen, including the guy running Tombstone, was, you know, what a lot of people don't know. Earp was, he was heavily invested in Tombstone. So he owned a lot of land. He owned a lot of buildings of which he was the landlord. He had interests in several of the businesses, some of which he won in card games. And so he was motivated to drive the criminal element from Tombstone. The gunfight at OK Corral was about protecting his investments. It was a total capitalist venture forced upon him because letting these morons take over the town was going to ruin all of his investments. He deserted his first wife in 1882 for his mistress, then remained faithful to her until his death in 1929, in part probably because she came from a very wealthy family. And the family became the source of his venture capital and most of his political connections. Just like John McCain is what I was going to say. In numerous interviews, yeah, McCain too, by the way. McCain actually delivered the divorce information to the first wife while she was in the hospital on a hospital bed. In numerous interviews, Earp defined himself as a businessman. And he pretty much moved from place to place where there was development going on, mining towns, cattle towns, and became a law man so he could legally carry a gun to protect his business investments. For a handful of years he was really wealthy, but ultimately wound up broke and lived off his reputation for years doing personal appearances for money and for room and board. All right. And in 1911, at age 63, he was arrested in Los Angeles for vagrancy and trying to con a tourist out of $25,000. Died completely broke. He was not, as you pointed out, the fastest gun in the West. In fact, he was probably one of the slowest guns in the West and had he not hit a couple of fast gun guys around him. He'd have been dead long before he got arrested for vagrancy. Well, what's the point? Well, the point is that legends rarely reflect reality. And that legends need to be relatively simple for people to grasp, remember, and repeat. So nobody's going to memorize the biography. They're going to get the capsule of it that you create. And so what served him well was perceived as a law man, perceived as fastest gun in the West, probably discouraged a lot of people from trying him, therefore diminishing the number of times he had to draw a gun. The law man thing made him acceptable to political interests. And now we remember him based on the legend that he deliberately tried to create. I'm reading a really good biography right now of Houdini. I meant to bring it this morning and I didn't, but I'm bringing it into my office. I'm just into it this much, but it's clear. It's already one that you should read. And it is also clear that he went to great extremes to create a particular legend that did not in any way, shape, or form reflect reality, but is what he became known for. And so this is about not only what are you known for, but what are you known for in a way that people can remember it and repeat it and retell it to others. And so everybody within the context of their personal narrative needs to think all the way through to legend. I've given you
